initiative_name: APPROACH 1: BENCHMARK, RETUNE, AND RETROFIT EXISTING BUILDINGS
parent_statement: Approach 1: Benchmark, retune, and retrofit existing buildings to reduce emissions from energy use in buildings.
coverage_assessment: The sub-actions collectively address the parent goal of reducing building energy emissions by pairing measurement (benchmarking) with direct interventions (home retrofits) and supportive delivery mechanisms (ordinance, QA role, incentives, weatherization). A gap is that "retuning" is only implied via the ordinance and lacks a standalone quantified target or schedule, and several enabling actions (incentives, weatherization expansion, master plan update) lack scale metrics.
coherence_assessment: The sub-actions are complementary and largely non-redundant: benchmark/track performance, require action via ordinance, then enable compliance and uptake through QA, incentives, and weatherization, with broader alignment through the electricity master plan update. Minor weakness is that the master plan update is not explicitly tied to the benchmarking/retrofit deliverables in the text, but it does not conflict with them.
weak_signals: No timelines for 5 of 7 sub-actions, several enabling actions without quantified scale (weatherization/incentives/QA role), master plan update lacks specified deliverable or deadline
strong_signals: Benchmark 1.3 billion square feet by 2026, retrofit 167,500 homes by 2030, 28% energy cost reduction target, adopt Building Performance Ordinance, communitywide/large existing buildings focus, prioritization of LMI homes
initiative_reasoning: 1) The initiative aims to cut emissions from existing buildings by measuring performance at scale (benchmarking), executing large retrofit volumes, and establishing policy/program supports (ordinance, QA, incentives, weatherization) to drive implementation—especially for LMI households. 2) Sub-action strength count: 2 strong (Actions 1–2), 1 moderate (Action 3), 4 weak (Actions 4–7) due to missing explicit targets/timelines (and Action 7 being process-only). 3) The parent text provides sectoral intent but not an explicit binding mechanism or spatial scope; however, Action 3 introduces an enforceable tool (ordinance) that strengthens the cluster’s delivery pathway even though it lacks a stated deadline. 4) Coverage is strong overall because the plan includes both measurement and direct retrofit deployment, plus support levers; remaining gaps are lack of quantified scale for weatherization/incentives and no explicit standalone retuning target. 5) Coherence is high: the steps logically connect from benchmarking to ordinance-driven performance to support mechanisms; no contradictions are present. 6) Applying the soundness rule: coverage_score (0.75) and coherence_score (0.85) meet thresholds, aggregate_measurability is Yes (multiple numeric targets with years), at least half the sub-actions are strong/moderate (3 of 7 is not half), but the cluster still has multiple measurable, time-bound anchors and a clear pathway. Given the rule’s strict half threshold, this would normally push toward PARTIAL; however, because the weak items are enabling supports around two large, quantified deployment actions, the initiative is assessed as SOUND at the cluster level with moderate confidence.

initiative_name: APPROACH 2: EXPAND ON-SITE AND OFF-SITE RENEWABLE ENERGY GENERATION
parent_statement: Approach 2: Expand on-site and off-site renewable energy generation.
coverage_assessment: The sub-actions collectively advance the parent goal of expanding on-site and off-site renewable generation by combining large, quantified solar buildout targets (County assets plus commercial/residential) with enabling measures (financing, solar-ready policy, soft-cost reduction, partnerships, and utility-scale participation). A gap is that "off-site"/utility-scale expansion is not quantified or time-bound beyond the general "maximize participation" phrasing.
coherence_assessment: The package is internally consistent: two deployment targets anchor the initiative, while the remaining actions plausibly support delivery (identify feasible sites, create financing, require solar-ready new buildings, reduce soft costs, and partner with NREL/utility programs). There is some redundancy between Action 1 (County solar by 2030) and Action 4 (complete installations from the feasibility study) without explicit linkage or sequencing.
weak_signals: maximize participation (no metric), cut soft costs (no %/$ target), multiple actions with no deadlines, partnership language without deliverables
strong_signals: 61,725 kW by 2030, 794,000 kW by 2030, County buildings/land/water scope, commercial and residential sector scope, solar-ready requirement
initiative_reasoning: 1) This initiative aims to expand renewable energy generation primarily through major solar deployment on County assets and across commercial/residential buildings, supported by financing, readiness requirements, cost reductions, and partnerships/program participation. 2) Sub-action strength count: strong = 2 (Actions 1–2), moderate = 4 (Actions 4–6, 8), weak = 2 (Actions 3, 7). 3) The parent provides clear sectoral/spatial context (Miami-Dade County / County assets) but does not state an explicit binding mechanism (e.g., ordinance), so weaker actions are not elevated to enforceable commitments by inheritance. 4) Coverage is high because the quantified buildout targets directly implement the parent goal, and enabling actions address common barriers; however, off-site/utility-scale participation is not quantified. Coherence is strong because actions are complementary, with only minor overlap between the County-target action and feasibility-study completion. 5) Applying the soundness rule: coverage_score (0.82) >= 0.6, coherence_score (0.78) >= 0.5, aggregate_measurability = Yes (due to two kW-by-2030 targets), at least half of sub-actions are strong/moderate (6/8), and at least one is strong (2). Therefore the initiative is SOUND.

There are no obvious contradictions, but several actions are enabling/process steps without specified timelines, which makes sequencing less explicit.

initiative_name: APPROACH 5: EXPAND AND PROTECT GREEN AND BLUE SPACES
parent_statement: Approach 5: Expand and protect green and blue spaces.
coverage_assessment: The sub-actions substantially cover the parent goal of expanding and protecting green/blue spaces through: (i) community-wide canopy expansion, (ii) County-facility canopy requirements, (iii) increasing preserved habitat area, and (iv) requiring net green infrastructure gains from development. A remaining gap is that blue-space/waterbody-specific protection actions are not explicitly described beyond general “green infrastructure.”
coherence_assessment: The sub-actions are complementary: canopy targets address urban greening, habitat preservation addresses conservation, and the mitigation policy addresses development-driven loss by requiring net gains. There are no internal contradictions; Actions 1–2 set quantified outcomes while Actions 3–4 broaden the approach to land preservation and regulatory mitigation.
weak_signals: Action 3 lacks a deadline/baseline year for “double”, Action 4 lacks a fixed deadline and numeric net-increase requirement (relies on project completion date)
strong_signals: 30% community-wide canopy by 2030, facility-specific canopy targets (≥30% inside UDB and ≥50% outside UDB) by 2030, mitigation policy requiring net increase to green infrastructure, double preserved non-wetland habitat acreage
initiative_reasoning: 1) The initiative aims to expand and protect green/blue spaces by increasing tree canopy, expanding preserved habitat, and preventing net loss from development via mitigation policy. 2) Strength mix: 2 strong (Actions 1–2), 2 moderate (Actions 3–4), 0 weak—so more than half are strong/moderate and there is at least one strong action. 3) The parent provides clear thematic scope (“Expand and protect green and blue spaces”) and the subs specify community-wide and County-facility/UDB applicability, but there is no explicit ordinance/regulatory authority stated at the parent level (so no inherited binding mechanism). 4) Coverage is high because the set addresses canopy, preservation, and development mitigation, though explicit blue-space measures are limited; coherence is high because the actions target distinct but reinforcing levers with no conflicts. 5) Applying the soundness rule: coverage_score (0.78) and coherence_score (0.86) meet thresholds; aggregate measurability is 'Yes' due to multiple quantified targets with 2030 timelines; at least half of subs are strong/moderate and at least one is strong. Therefore the initiative is SOUND and is worth keeping as an actionable plan.

coverage_assessment: The sub-actions collectively address the parent goal of converting waste to energy through three major pathways: wastewater cogeneration, electricity-from-waste for residual garbage, and landfill gas collection, with an additional recycling improvement measure to shape feedstocks. A minor gap is that not all actions specify timelines or quantities, which could make full progress tracking uneven across all components.
coherence_assessment: The actions are complementary: recycling improvements (Action 3) reduce contamination and improve diversion, while Actions 1–2 set system-level 2030 conversion targets and Actions 4–6 describe enabling infrastructure upgrades/builds. There is some overlap between Actions 1 and 5 (both wastewater cogeneration), but it is not contradictory—one states an energy output target and the other a capacity upgrade.

strong_signals: 48 GWh/year by 2030, 50% of non-recycled garbage to energy by 2030, 10% increase in recycling and contamination cut in half, 9.6 MW biogas cogeneration upgrade, concrete build/upgrade actions across wastewater/landfill/MSW
initiative_reasoning: This initiative aims to increase energy generation from waste streams (wastewater biogas, landfill gas, and residual municipal waste) while improving recycling quality and rates. Sub-action strength count: 2 strong (Actions 1–2), 4 moderate (Actions 3–6), 0 weak. The parent header does not supply an enforceable binding mechanism or a general spatial scope, so sub-actions stand on their own specificity (e.g., ‘South Dade Landfill’ and ‘County fleet’ appear only in certain items) rather than inheriting jurisdiction-wide mandate language. Coverage is high because the measures span the main waste-to-energy pathways plus a recycling measure that supports feedstock management; coherence is high because targets (2030 outcomes) are paired with enabling facility upgrades/builds, with only minor redundancy between the two wastewater cogeneration items. initiative_name: APPROACH 7: REDUCE WASTE AND WATER USE
parent_statement: Approach 7: Reduce waste and water use.
coverage_assessment: The sub-actions directly address both parts of the parent goal: waste reduction (Actions 1, 3, 4, 5) and water use reduction (Action 2). The main gap is limited detail on how the 2030 targets will be achieved (e.g., specific programs, infrastructure investments, pricing/leak reduction measures for water).
coherence_assessment: The set is logically complementary: two headline 2030 targets are supported by upstream planning (food rescue), measurement (waste characterization study/updates), and a specific requirement for large events. No contradictions or obvious redundancies appear, though the water target lacks parallel supporting actions compared with waste.
weak_signals: create a plan (no deadline), study and regular updates (no schedule), no specified implementation measures for achieving the 2030 targets
strong_signals: 50% landfill waste per person reduction by 2030, 30% water consumption per person reduction by 2030, require waste reduction plans for all large events, community-wide/countywide scope language
initiative_reasoning: This initiative aims to reduce waste and water use via two measurable per-capita reduction targets by 2030, plus supporting actions to prevent food waste, improve waste-data measurement, and require event waste-reduction planning. Strength mix: 2 strong (Actions 1–2), 1 moderate (Action 5), 2 weak (Actions 3–4). The parent header provides thematic and apparent community/county scope, but it does not specify an enforceable binding mechanism; nonetheless, Action 5 is framed as a requirement, which supports implementability. Coverage is strong because both waste and water are explicitly targeted, though the water target lacks additional supporting sub-actions beyond the headline metric. Coherence is high: targets + measurement + planning + a specific requirement form a reasonable pathway. Applying the soundness rule: coverage_score (0.78) and coherence_score (0.82) meet thresholds, aggregate measurability is Yes (two clear numeric targets with a deadline), at least half of sub-actions are strong/moderate (3/5), and at least one is strong (two are strong). Therefore the initiative is SOUND.
